ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general hamburger class; no dedicated vegetarian burger class exists. Closest defensible match used. Vegetarian patty composition (mixed vegetables, potato, cottage cheese) is not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers vegetable-based burgers as a category. Exact Indian-style veg burger with paneer/potato patty is not a distinct FoodEx2 entry; this is the closest defensible code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for vegetarian burger found in standard release; null applied.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 280 g, comprising one assembled veg burger (bun, patty, sauces, onions) plus a side of french fries.

nutrient_source.title: Veg Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Veg Burger (cr_id G00010653). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White bread / burger bun analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for an Indian-style veg burger. GI of 65 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being a white wheat burger bun (GI ~65–70 per University of Sydney GI Database for white bread/bun products). The vegetable-potato-paneer patty and sauces are secondary contributors. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 29.57 × 2.8) / 100 ≈ 47.9 for a 280 g serving (carbohydrate per serving ≈ 82.8 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (197 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (487 mg/100 g); if consumed, request reduced sauce, avoid added salt, and pair with a potassium-rich side such as avocado or leafy greens to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (29.57 g/100 g) from white bun with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g), yielding a medium-to-high glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish sparingly; substituting a wholegrain bun, reducing portion size, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help blunt the postprandial glucose spike associated with the refined white bun and sweet chilli sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (487 mg/100 g), moderate fat (10.18 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g); cholesterol 29 mg/100 g is modest given vegetarian patty
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, this dish is best consumed occasionally and in reduced portions; choosing a wholegrain bun, minimising high-sodium sauces, and increasing vegetable accompaniments will improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g below 3 g threshold), borderline-high sodium, and refined carbohydrate from white bun contributing pro-inflammatory load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer the vegetable patty without the refined bun, request grilled rather than pan-fried preparation, and monitor response to nightshade ingredients such as tomato; reducing sauce quantity will also lower the pro-inflammatory sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Burger is a popular Indian-influenced vegetarian fast-food dish served widely in Singapore. It consists of a homemade vegetarian patty made from a mixture of mixed vegetables, potato, and cottage cheese (paneer), pan-fried until golden, and assembled in a soft white wheat burger bun. The burger is topped with mint chutney sauce, sweet chilli sauce, and sliced onions, and is served alongside a portion of french fries. The patty delivers a soft, mildly spiced interior with a lightly crisp exterior, while the mint and sweet chilli sauces provide contrasting cooling and tangy-sweet flavour notes. Nutritionally, the dish provides 263 kcal per 100 g, with 13.3 g protein (from paneer and vegetables), 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate dominated by the refined white bun. Dietary fibre is low at 1.8 g/100 g and sodium is elevated at 487 mg/100 g, making this dish a moderate-to-high concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is fully vegetarian and contains dairy (paneer) and gluten (bun). It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner item and is found in Indian fast-food stalls and canteens across Singapore. Substituting a wholegrain bun and reducing sauce quantity would substantially improve its nutritional profile for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: Is veg burger good for diabetics?
Is veg burger suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Veg Burger (cr_id G00010653) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white burger bun as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, clearly documented.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010653 as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo source record for this dish. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 13.3, fat_g 10.18, carbohydrate_g 29.57) are non-zero. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat burger bun (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI entry exists for Indian-style veg burger. Halal status assumed based on vegetarian composition (no meat, no alcohol-based ingredients); formal halal certification not confirmed from source.
